div.p: Joenja 70 mg film‑coated tablets
Each film‑coated tablet contains leniolisib phosphate equivalent to 70 mg leniolisib.
Each tablet contains 241.16 mg of lactose monohydrate.
For the full list of excipients, see section 6.1.
Film‑coated tablet.
Yellow, oval‑shaped, biconvex, bevelled edge film‑coated tablet debossed with “70” on one side and “LNB” on the other side.
Joenja is indicated for the treatment of activated phosphoinositide 3‑kinase delta syndrome (APDS) in adults and adolescents 12 years of age and older.
Treatment must be initiated by a physician experienced in the management of primary immune deficiencies.
The recommended dose in adult and adolescent patients who weigh more than 45 kg is 70 mg leniolisib twice daily approximately 12 hours apart.
If a dose is missed by more than 6 hours, the patient should not take the missed dose but resume dosing at the next scheduled time.
If vomiting occurs within 1 hour after taking Joenja, take another Joenja tablet as soon as possible. If vomiting occurs more than 1 hour after dosing, do not take an additional dose.
The safety and efficacy of leniolisib in patients over the age of 65 years have not been established. No data are available.
Leniolisib has not been studied in patients with renal impairment.
Leniolisib has not been studied in patients with hepatic impairment. Use of Joenja in patients with moderate to severe hepatic impairment is not recommended.
The safety and efficacy of Joenja in children aged less than 12 years or below the weight of 45 kg have not yet been established. No data are available.
Oral use.
The film‑coated tablet can be taken with or without food (see section 5.2).
Hypersensitivity to the active substance or to any of the excipients listed in section 6.1.

title.value: 4.4 Special warnings and precautions for use

display.value: Special warnings and precautions for use

div.h2: 4.4 Special warnings and precautions for use
div.p: Concomitant therapy with strong and moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors increased leniolisib exposure. Co-administered with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors should be avoided. If use of moderate or strong CYP3A4 inhibitors are required, it is recommended that Joenja be discontinued 2 days before administration of CYP3A4 inhibitor. Joenja may be restarted 7 days after CYP3A inhibitor discontinuation (see section 4.5).

p.u: Combination with CYP1A2 metabolised drugs with a narrow therapeutic index

div.p: Time-dependent inhibition of CYP1A2 was observed in the presence of leniolisib in vitro . Caution is advised if Joenja is used with drugs that are primarily metabolised by CYP1A2 and have a narrow therapeutic index (NTI). It is recommended to interrupt Joenja dosing for at least 2 days prior to initiating therapy with an NTI CYP1A2 substrate. Restart Joenja after 7 days of an adequate washout of the NTI CYP1A2 substrate (see section 4.5).

p.u: Combination with BCRP, OATP1B1, and OATP1B3 substrates

div.p: The effect of leniolisib on BCRP, OATP1B1, and OATP1B3 substrates has not been studied clinically; however, an increase in systemic exposure of drugs that are substrates of these transporters cannot be excluded. Co-administration should be avoided.
For patients using antacids chronically, the antacid should be taken either 2 hours before or 2 hours after Joenja administration.
Women of childbearing potential must use highly effective contraception while taking Joenja (see section 4.6).
This medicine contains lactose. Patients with rare hereditary problems of galactose intolerance, total lactase deficiency, or glucose‑galactose malabsorption must not take this medicine.
This medicine contains less than 1 mmol sodium (23 mg) per film-coated tablet, that is to say essentially ‘sodium‑free’.

title.value: 4.5 Interaction with other medicinal products and other forms of interaction

div.h2: 4.5 Interaction with other medicinal products and other forms of interaction

p.u: Medicinal products affecting the pharmacokinetics of leniolisib

div.p: Leniolisib is cleared primarily through oxidative metabolism (primarily hydroxylation and dealkylation) by cytochrome P450 isoenzymes (predominantly CYP3A4, 95.4%). In a study of healthy adults, co‑administration of leniolisib and itraconazole, a strong CYP3A inhibitor, resulted in a 2‑fold increase in leniolisib exposure. Concomitant use of Joenja with strong CYP3A inhibitors should be avoided (see section 4.4).
Although co‑administration of leniolisib with strong and moderate CYP3A inducers decreased leniolisib exposure, no loss of efficacy is anticipated.

p.u: Combination with CYP1A2 metabolized drugs with a narrow therapeutic index

div.p: Time-dependent (irreversible) inhibition of CYP1A2 was observed in the presence of leniolisib in vitro . Clinically relevant interactions at the therapeutic dose of leniolisib (70 mg twice daily) could not be excluded for inhibition of CYP1A2.

p.u: Drugs that may have their exposure altered by leniolisib

div.p: The effect of leniolisib on BCRP, OATP1B1, and OATP1B3 substrates has not been studied clinically. In vitro , leniolisib is a substrate and an inhibitor of the hepatic efflux transporters OATP1B1/B3 and BCRP and is a substrate of P‑gp. Although the likelihood of an in vivo inhibition is low when co-administered with leniolisib, an increase in systemic exposure of drugs that are substrates of these transporters cannot be excluded. Co-administration should be avoided. As leniolisib is marginally renally excreted (6%), the observed in vitro inhibition on renal uptake transporters is not considered clinically relevant.

Leniolisib exhibits pH-dependent solubility, with lower solubility at higher pH-values. Avoid co-administration of Joenja with locally acting antacids. Antacids should be taken either 2 hours before or 2 hours after Joenja administration.

Administration of leniolisib with a single dose oral contraceptive containing ethinylestradiol and levonorgestrel increased ethinylestradiol exposure by approximately 30% with no effect on levonorgestrel exposure. The increase in ethinylestradiol exposure is unlikely to reduce the effectiveness of a combined oral contraceptive composed of ethinylestradiol and levonorgestrel.
No vaccine interaction studies have been performed using Joenja. Vaccinations may be less effective if administered during Joenja treatment.
Interaction studies have only been performed in adults.
Based on findings in animals, women of childbearing potential must use highly effective methods of contraception during treatment with Joenja and for 1 week after the last dose (see section 5.3).

There are no data from the use of leniolisib in pregnant women. Embryonic and foetal development toxicity studies showed teratogenic effects of leniolisib in rats and rabbits
(see section 5.3). Joenja should be discontinued during pregnancy or in women of childbearing potential not using highly effective methods of contraception.

It is unknown whether leniolisib and its metabolites are excreted in human milk. A risk to
newborn infants/infants and toddlers cannot be excluded. Breast‑feeding should be discontinued during treatment with Joenja.

No human data on the effect of leniolisib on fertility are available. In rats, slight effects on the male reproductive organs were observed and were partially to fully reversible within a 4-week recovery period. These effects did not reduce fertility indices or pregnancy rates (see section 5.3).

title.value: 4.7 Effects on ability to drive and use machines

div.h2: 4.7 Effects on ability to drive and use machines
div.p: Joenja has no or negligible influence on the ability to drive and use machines.

Safety data for leniolisib in APDS patients were obtained from two studies, Study 2201, a 2-part study and Study 2201E1. Study 2201 Part 1 (N=6) was an open‑label, within‑patient, up-titration, dose‑finding study evaluating leniolisib 10 mg, 30 mg, and 70 mg twice daily for 4 weeks per dose level to establish the safety and pharmacokinetics of leniolisib, as well as to select the optimal dose for use in Part 2. No patients from Part 1 were enrolled in Part 2
. Part 2 was a 12‑week randomized, blinded, placebo‑controlled study of leniolisib 70 mg twice daily in 31 patients. Twenty‑one patients were treated with leniolisib, and 10 received placebo. Additional safety data was obtained from Study 2201E1, a long-term open-label extension study in 37 patients. A total of 38 unique APDS patients received leniolisib in these clinical trials.
The most commonly reported adverse reactions during leniolisib treatment were weight increased, atopic dermatitis, fatigue, dyspepsia, vomiting, and headache.
No significant differences in the incidence of adverse reactions were observed for APDS patients by disease type, age, or gender.
No adverse drug reactions were reported in Study 2201 Part 1.

Table 1 lists the adverse drug reactions reported with leniolisib in Study 2201 Part 2 and Study 2201E1. Listed adverse reactions are divided into groups according to the MedDRA system organ class and frequency. Frequencies are defined as follows: very common (≥1/10), common (≥1/100 to <1/10), uncommon (≥1/1,000 to <1/100), rare (≥1/10,000 to <1/1,000), and not known (cannot be estimated from the available data). Within each frequency grouping, adverse reactions are presented in order of decreasing seriousness.

p.strong: Table 1 Adverse drug reactions reported in clinical studies

td.p: General disorders and administration site conditions

div.p:
Reporting suspected adverse reactions after authorisation of the medicinal product is important. It allows continued monitoring of the benefit/risk balance of the medicinal product. Healthcare professionals are asked to report any suspected adverse reactions via the national reporting system listed in
Appendix V
.

If overdose occurs, the patient must be monitored for evidence of toxicity (see section 4.8). Treatment of overdose with Joenja consists of general supportive measures including monitoring of vital signs as well as observation of the clinical status of the patient.

Pharmacotherapeutic group:
{group}, ATC code: {code} not yet assigned

p.u: Mechanism of action and Pharmacodynamic effects

div.p: Leniolisib is an orally bioavailable small-molecule inhibitor of p110δ that selectively inhibits the PI3Kδ enzyme. This disease-modifying mechanism of action inhibits the key signalling pathway that leads to increased production of PIP3, hyperactivity of the PI3K/ pathway, and subsequent deficiencies and dysregulation of B and T cells that give rise to the main clinical phenotypes of the activated phosphoinositide 3-kinase syndrome (APDS). Leniolisib has a 28-fold greater selectivity for PI3Kδ over PI3Kα, a 43-fold greater for PI3Kδ over PI3Kβ, and 257-fold greater selectivity for PI3Kδ over PI3Kγ. Leniolisib reduces phosphorylated AKT (pAKT) pathway overactivity in APDS-affected B and T cells, ex vivo .

Joenja was evaluated in a single study (Study 2201) in APDS patients with genetically activated PI3K. The study consisted of two parts. Part 1 (N=6) was an open‑label, within‑patient dose‑finding study designed to establish the safety and pharmacokinetics of leniolisib in the target APDS population, as well as to select the optimal dose to be tested in Part 2. Part 2 was a 12-week randomised, blinded, placebo-controlled study of leniolisib 70 mg twice daily in 31 patients to assess the efficacy and safety of leniolisib in the target APDS population. Eligible patients were able to continue in a long-term safety extension study (Study 2201E1).

Study 2201 recruited patients aged 12 to 75 years in Europe and North America. A total of 32 patients were screened and 31 were randomized 2:1 to receive either leniolisib 70 mg (N=21) or placebo (N=10) twice a day for 12 weeks.

In Study 2201 Part 2, the co-primary endpoints were the improvement in immune dysregulation, as measured by change from baseline in the log10‑transformed sum of product diameters (SPD) of index lesions, and the normalisation of immunophenotype as measured by the percentage of naïve B cells out of total B cells. The difference in adjusted mean change in log10-transformed SPD for leniolisib (N=18) compared to placebo (N=8) was -0.24 (CI95, -0.37, -0.11; p=0.0012). The difference in adjusted mean change in percent naïve B cells for leniolisib (N=8) compared to placebo (N=5) was 40.13 (CI95, 28.51, 51.75; p<0.0001).
Improvement in both co‑primary efficacy measures was also statistically and clinically significant when adjusting for use of IgG at Baseline. Results are presented in Table 2 below:

th.p: Table 2 Primary Analysis of Change from Baseline at Week 12 (Day 85), Pharmacodynamic Analysis Set

p.strong: Log 10 ‑transformed SPD of Index Lesions (excluding patients with 0 lesions at Baseline, or SPD=0 at Day 85)
Percentage of naïve B cells out of total B cells (patients with <48% of naïve B cells at Baseline)

div.p: Secondary endpoints supported the primary endpoints. Log10-transformed 3D volume of measurable non-index lesions had significantly greater reductions with leniolisib than with placebo (p=0.0033) although change in log10-transformed 3D volume of index lesions were similar (excluding patients with 0 lesions at baseline, or 100% reduction in 3D volume at day 85). A reduction in the volume and bi-dimensional size of the spleen was statistically greater with leniolisib than with placebo (p=0.0020 and p=0.0148, respectively).

Patient-reported outcomes were similar between leniolisib and placebo after 12 weeks of treatment. Mean improvement in patient-reported outcomes (quality of life as measured by Short Form 36 (SF‑36), APDS-related well-being as measured by Patient’s Global Assessment-Visual analogue scales (PtGA), and Work Productivity Activity Impairment plus Classroom Impairment Questionnaire (WPAI)) exceeded meaningful change thresholds by one year of therapy.
No increase in cytomegalovirus (CMV) or Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) viremia markers were observed during treatment.

Exploratory analysis of serum immunoglobulin M (IgM) demonstrated levels in individual patients decreasing between 24 to 54% by the end of the 12-week treatment period for a mean change of ‑33.52% in the leniolisib group vs 2.99% in the placebo group. A post hoc external comparison of IgM levels between Study 2201E1 and current practice (physician’s choice of treatment, in some cases including immunosuppressants such as sirolimus) in the European Society of Immunodeficiency Registry, balanced using inverse probability of treatment weighting, demonstrated a significantly greater (p=0.0012) reduction in IgM with leniolisib compared with current practice.

Thirty-five of the patients enrolled in Study 2201 Parts 1 and 2, and two additional patients not treated in Study 2201 but previously treated with a different PI3Kδ inhibitor, continued in Study 2201E1 and were evaluable for maintenance of response. Mean duration of leniolisib treatment was approximately 2 years, and 3 patients had more than 5 years of leniolisib exposure. There was a durable effect in continued reduction of lymphoproliferation and increases in naïve to total B cells and decrease and then levelling off of transitional B cells from Baseline to Week 36. Additionally, there were clinically meaningful reductions in infection rates (p=0.0040), accompanied by post hoc observation of reduction in immunoglobulin replacement therapy.

For adolescents aged 12‑17 years, please see above. The European Medicines Agency has deferred the obligation to submit the results of studies with leniolisib in one or more subsets of the paediatric population in APDS (see section 4.2 for information on paediatric use).

In a placebo‑controlled, ascending single and multiple dose study in healthy participants, leniolisib was rapidly absorbed in the fasted state, with median time to maximum concentration in the plasma (t max ) at about 1 hour post dose. T max appeared independent of dose and was not altered after multiple oral doses.

Co‑administration of a single 70 mg dose of leniolisib with a high fat meal prolonged the rate of absorption (T max approximately 4 hours) and decreased C max on average by 41% but not the extent of absorption (AUC). The impact of the food effect on pAKT inhibition is expected to be minor and not leading to lower average or trough inhibition. Patients may therefore take Joenja irrespective of food.

The systemic decay in leniolisib plasma concentration over time is bi‑exponential, indicating a distribution delay towards peripheral tissues. The apparent terminal elimination t 1/2 is approximately 10 hours (estimate from steady‑state drug washout). The median oral volume of distribution during the terminal phase is 42 L (range of 24 L to 128 L), indicating that leniolisib has a moderate‑to‑low volume of distribution.

Leniolisib was 60% metabolized by the liver, with CYP3A4 as the most predominant enzyme involved (95.4%) in the primary oxidative metabolism of leniolisib, with minor contribution from other enzymes (3.5% CYP3A5, 0.7% CYP1A2 and 0.4% CYP2D6). The strong activity of recombinant CYP1A1 suggests a possible involvement of this enzyme in the biotransformation of leniolisib in extra‑hepatic tissues. Intestinal secretion by BCRP and extrahepatic CYP1A1 cannot be excluded as excretion routes.
The mass balance of an oral dose of 70 mg 14 C‑leniolisib was 92.5% (standard deviation: 2.3%) 168 hours post dose (morning of Day 8).
14 C‑leniolisib was excreted predominately via faeces (67.0%), while excretion via urine was approximately 25.5%. Approximately 70% of the 14 C‑leniolisib was recovered within 48 hours.

In vitro , leniolisib is a substrate and an inhibitor of the hepatic efflux transporter BCRP and a substrate of P‑gp. Leniolisib was identified in vitro as a weak potential inhibitor of the hepatic uptake and efflux transporters OATP1B1/B3 and BCRP.

Leniolisib exposure was increased approximately 2‑fold when administered with itraconazole (strong CYP3A4 inhibitor) whereas quinidine (strong P‑gp and CYP2D6 inhibitor) had no effect. Leniolisib is not a sensitive substrate of P‑gp and CYP2D6.

When combined with a monophasic oral contraceptive containing levonorgestrel and ethinylestradiol, leniolisib increased ethinylestradiol exposure by approximately 25 to 30% in terms of both AUC and C max but did not affect the C max or AUC of levonorgestrel (90% CI of geometric mean: 0.80 to 1.25). The efficacy of a combined oral contraceptive composed of ethinylestradiol and levonorgestrel is not expected to be compromised by concomitant use of leniolisib.

A clear dose and concentration dependency in drug effect was seen over the leniolisib dose range explored. Although a single dose of leniolisib 10 mg generally reduced the ratio of pAKT positive (pAKT(S473) + B cells after ex vivo stimulation) cells to less than 20%, the inhibition generally waned over the next 12 hours. A slightly more pronounced peak drug effect and more sustained inhibition were seen at higher doses. Although 30 and 70 mg did not greatly differ in their extent of maximum effect, inhibition was clearly more sustained over time at 70 mg, which may therefore be most optimal in terms of time‑average inhibition as well as inhibition at trough
(12 hours post dose).

Scatterplots of the primary efficacy endpoints (Day 85) change in
log 10 SPD of index lesions and change in naïve B cells vs Day 1 AUC from the first dose until last assessment point (AUC last ) showed a slight trend for larger change in index lesions with increasing AUC last with no indication for correlation between change in naïve B cells and drug exposure.

Leniolisib did not show mutagenic, clastogenic, or aneugenic potential in the genotoxicity studies, i.e., the in vitro microbial reverse mutation (Ames) assay, the in vitro micronucleus assay in TK6 cells, chromosomal aberration assay in human peripheral blood lymphocytes, or in vivo micronucleus study in rats. No signs of carcinogenic potential (e.g., hyperplasia/neoplasia) were found in chronic nonclinical studies. Long‑term animal studies to evaluate the carcinogenic potential of leniolisib have not been conducted.

Slight effects on the male reproductive organs (prostate, testes, and epididymis) were observed in rats. In the 26-week repeat dose rat study, lower prostate weights correlated with a decreased secretion seen microscopically. In this study and the 10‑week juvenile rat study, lower testes and epididymis weights and lower sperm counts were linked to decreases in the germinal epithelium and round spermatids and loss of spermatocytes. The minimal histological findings occurred only in occasional males at 90 and 120 mg/kg/day with no effects on mating, fertility indices, or pregnancy rates. In the 26-week study, these effects were observed in the high dose group (90 mg/kg/day) with similar incidence and severity with no progression by increasing the treatment duration. The described histological changes were limited to studies in rats and were not observed in studies in monkeys with up to 39 weeks of exposure. Therefore, reduced male fertility is not expected to be clinically relevant.

Embryonic and foetal development studies in rats and rabbits showed microphthalmia (rats and rabbits) as well as reduced orbital socket size and anophthalmia (rats only) at the highest dose levels (120 and 100 mg/kg/day, respectively). In rats, the maternal exposure at the NOAEL of 30 mg/kg/day was 2.6 times the human exposure based on steady state AUC 0‑24h unbound at the recommended dose of 70 mg twice a day (see section 4.6).
Lactose monohydrate
Microcrystalline cellulose
Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose
Sodium starch glycolate (Type A)
Magnesium stearate
Colloidal silicon dioxide
Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose
Titanium dioxide
Iron oxide yellow
Iron oxide red
Talc
Polyethylene glycol
Not applicable.
24 months
Do not store above 25°C. Do not refrigerate.
High density polyethylene bottles with aluminium induction seal and child resistant polypropylene screw cap.
Each pack contains 1 bottle with 60 tablets.
No special requirements.
Any unused medicinal product or waste material should be disposed of in accordance with local requirements.
Pharming Technologies B.V.
Darwinweg 24
2333 CR Leiden
The Netherlands

title.value: 9. DATE OF FIRST AUTHORISATION/RENEWAL OF THE AUTHORISATION

div.h2: 9. DATE OF FIRST AUTHORISATION/RENEWAL OF THE AUTHORISATION
div.p: Date of first authorisation: {DD month YYYY}

title.value: 10. DATE OF REVISION OF THE TEXT

div.h2: 10. DATE OF REVISION OF THE TEXT
div.p:
Detailed information on this medicinal product is available on the website of the European Medicines Agency
http://www.ema.europa.eu
.
